PREFACE 
 
The advent of the Competence-Based Curriculum (CBC) heralds a new era of dynamic and 
practical learning experiences designed to equip learners with the 21st century skills. 
 
The choice of the Competence-Based Curriculum (CBC) marks a transformative step in 
improving education quality and relevance. The Ministry of Education understands the 
challenges that come with transitioning to a new curriculum and is; therefore, committed to 
ensuring provision of quality teaching and learning materials to support effective lesson delivery. 
 
The English Language Teaching Module has been shaped in line with the Competence Based 
Education principles which seek to link education to real life experiences. The English Language 
Teaching Module fully embraces the General Competences provided in the 2023 Zambia 
Curriculum Framework such as collaboration, critical thinking, creativity and innovation, 
problem 
solving, 
analytical 
thinking, 
emotional 
intelligence, 
digital 
literacy 
and 
entrepreneurship. Effective implementation of the CBC requires clarity of focus, undivided 
dedication, commitment and setting higher level competences.  
 
It is my sincere hope that this CBC English Language Teaching Module for Form 1, will greatly 
improve the quality of education provided to English Language as outlined and emphasized in 
various policy documents; 'Education for Sustainability, 2023', and '2023 Zambia Education 
Curriculum Framework. 
 
This module reflects the Ministry's unwavering commitment to making this journey as smooth as 
possible for teachers, by providing structured guidance, suggested activities, and formative 
assessments that align with the CBC's objectives. However, teachers are encouraged to 
supplement this module with their own research and innovations in order to address any gaps 
that may arise during implementation. 
 
 
Kelvin Mambwe (PhD.) 
Permanent Secretary- Educational Services 
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

vi 
 
ACKOWLEDGEMENT 
 
The Form 1, Term 2 English Language Teaching Module is a result of wide consultation 
involving several stake holders within and outside the Education system. 
 
The Ministry of Education wishes to express its heartfelt gratitude for the valuable contributions

Finally, I acknowledge the dedication and hard work of the staff at the Curriculum Development 
Centre, whose efforts were instrumental in ensuring the successful completion of the English 
Language Teaching Module. 
 
 
Charles K. Ndakala (Dr.) 
Director Curriculum Development

vii 
 
INTRODUCTION 
 
The Form 1 Teaching Module has been meticulously designed to support teachers in the 
effective delivery of English Language lessons under the reformed curriculum. The module is 
developed based on the approved 2024 Competence-Based English Language Syllabus. It aims 
to bridge the gap created by the absence of approved textbooks and to equip educators with 
practical tools, activities, and assessments tailored to the learners' level and needs. 
 
The Ministry, understands the challenges that come with transitioning to a new curriculum that is 
Competence Based. The choice of the Competence-Based Curriculum (CBC) marks a 
transformative step in improving education quality and relevance. This module reflects the 
Ministry's unwavering commitment to making this journey as smooth as possible for teachers, by 
providing structured guidance, suggested activities, and formative assessments that align with the 
CBC's objectives. However, teachers are encouraged to supplement this module with their own 
research and innovations in order to address any gaps that may arise during implementation. 
 
The suggested activities and assessments provided in this module are designed to foster practical 
and critical thinking skills in learners while ensuring relevance and adaptability to different 
school contexts. Teachers are encouraged to tailor these suggestions to their unique classroom 
dynamics and to explore alternative, acceptable approaches when necessary. This flexibility is 
key to ensuring that all learners, regardless of their environment, benefit from meaningful and 
impactful Curriculum.  
 
It is envisaged that this module will serve as a valuable resource for teachers as they navigate the 
new Competence-Based Curriculum, enhancing their ability to inspire and equip learners with 
essential English Language knowledge, skills and positive attitudes desired to create a vibrant 
and responsive education system.

1 
 
CHAPTER 1: LIFE SKILLS AND HEALTH EDUCATION 
(LSHE) 
LISTENING AND SPEAKING 
TOPIC TITLE: PUBLIC SPEAKING 
1.0. INTRODUCTION  
 Overview: Public speaking is the art or skill of delivering a speech or presentation to 
an audience. It can take place in many settings like a classroom, at a conference, a 
business meeting or a social gathering. Public speaking is about communicating ideas 
effectively. Apart from words, body language, vocal tone and audience engagement 
are also essential for effective public speaking. This topic will cover debate. 
 General Competence(s):  
- 
Communication: Using appropriate language (verbal and non-verbal) in 
different situations. 
- 
Emotional Intelligence: communicate and relate effectively with others  
 Hook: Did you know that it takes a lot of effort to speak in public? 
 KEY TERMS /WORDS/VOCABULARY  
 Audience: the group of people you are speaking to 
 Tone: the emotion and emphasis behind the speaker’s voice 
 Voice Projection: speaking loudly and clearly so that everyone can hear 
 Persuasion: the ability to convince or influence an audience 
 Eye Contact: the act of looking at the audience to engage them and establish 
connection 
 Body Language: the non-verbal gestures, posture, and movements that communicate 
a message

2 
 
2.0. SUB-TOPIC – DEBATE 
 INTRODUCTION: A debate is a formal discussion where two parties present 
opposing views on a topic. In debate there are two sides, opposing and proposing. 
Debate helps develop critical thinking, persuasive speaking, and logical reasoning. 
Debates are common in education, politics and competitions to explore different 
views on important issues. This topic aims at equipping learners with skills to use in 
carrying out a debate.  
 SPECIFIC COMPETENCE: Use appropriate language to debate on different 
cross-cutting issues 
2.1 Learning Activities 
Activity 1: Identifying and discussing appropriate language for debate 
 Divide the class into two and ask the learners to debate a given motion 
For example: - Self-defence should be a compulsory part of life skills 
education 
- A healthy diet is more important than regular exercises 
 Lead learners into identifying appropriate language for debate and explain 
debate language as follows:  
- 
Introducing an argument (My main argument is that..., the evidence 
clearly suggests..., I firmly believe that... 
- 
Agreeing with a point (I completely support this idea because…, I 
agree with…., I concur…,)   
- 
Disagreeing Politely (I oppose the motion..., I see your point but I 
respectfully disagree..., I disagree...,) 
- 
Concluding an argument (To summarise my point..., In conclusion..., 
The evidence supports that…,) 
- 
In Parliamentary debate (Mr. Speaker, sir/madam may I take this 
opportunity to… I wish to interject…, I would like to bring to the 
attention of this house…) 
               
Activity 2: Using appropriate language to debate on cross-cutting issues: 
 organise the class to conduct a debate session on any of the following 
motions:  
- 
Is time management an inborn skill or one that is developed over time? 
- 
Should schools include mental health education in the curriculum? 
- 
A healthy diet is more important than regular exercise. 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials: 
 Video clips on debate 
 Worksheets/charts with appropriate language used in debate

3 
 
 Picture showing two panels of one proposing and the other opposing 
 
3.0. EXPECTED STANDARD: Language to debate on different cross cutting issues used      
correctly. 
Assessment:  
 Learners take turns to debate on different motions and are scored based on clarity of 
expression, tone of voice, voice projection, body language. 
Summary  
 Debate is a formal discussion where two parties present opposing views on a topic. 
 Debates are common in education, politics and competitions to explore different 
views on important issues. 
 In debate there are two sides, opposing and proposing. 
 Appropriate language is used in debate.  
 
TOPIC: COMPREHENSION 
1.0 INTRODUCTION  
 Overview: Comprehension is a process of active engagement that leads to accurate 
understanding and interpretation of what is heard or read. The importance of 
comprehension lies in its application to real-life situations such as understanding 
instructions, engaging in conversations, and interpreting information. Learners will read 
passages, extract main ideas, and engage in discussions to enhance their understanding. 
This topic introduces learners to the basic skills of comprehension, focusing on reading 
comprehension. 
 General Competence(s)  
- 
Communication: Apply good reading skills.  
- 
Critical Thinking: Analyse texts to improve the ability to understand.  
 
 Hook: Have you ever read something and felt like you understood every word, but still 
missed the deeper meaning? 
KEY TERMS /WORDS/VOCABULARY 
• Skimming: reading quickly to note only the important points  
• Scanning: reading carefully to find specific information

4 
 
• Factual: true, accurate, corresponding to reality 
• Inference: conclusion reached on the basis of evidence and reasoning  
 
2.0. SUB-TOPIC: INTENSIVE READING 
 INTRODUCTION: The ability to read with understanding allows a learner to 
understand other subjects in school. Learners need to develop skills in reading at a good 
speed, answering questions, recalling details and understanding meaning of unfamiliar 
words. This lesson gives an opportunity for the learner to practise and develop intensive 
reading skills. 
 SPECIFIC COMPETENCE: Read and comprehend information (Intensive Reading) 
 
2.1.LEARNING ACTIVITIES:  
Activity 1: Intensive reading and comprehending various pieces of 
information or texts:  
 
 Ask learners to skim through the passage to get the general idea 
 Time learners to ensure that they read at a speed of 200 words per minute.  
 Guide learners to avoid bad reading habits 
PASSAGE  
1. Stress is the body’s natural, human response to challenges or demands. It is a 
feeling of being under pressure, overwhelmed, or unable to cope with certain 
situations. Many different situations can cause stress, and this can change 
depending on our age. For instance, stress in teenagers may be caused by 
examinations, bullying, or difficult family situations. In comparison, young and 
older adults may struggle with stress relating to employment or significant events 
like becoming a parent and managing long-term health conditions.  
2. Stress in relationships can affect the way you think, feel, and behave. Stress in 
relationships can be as a result of arguments, family difficulties, illnesses, and 
bereavements. 
3. Stress can also be work related. For example, feeling a lack of control over your 
job, needing to continually work harder and for longer, worries about 
unemployment and not feeling rewarded for hard work can contribute to stress at 
work.

5 
 
4. Money and debt concerns are placing huge pressure on many of us, especially 
with the high cost of living. Many people are struggling to pay bills and grocery 
prices. To avoid money related stress and mental health difficulties, financial 
literacy comes in handy.  
5. When stressed, many people are tempted to turn to alcohol, nicotine, or other 
recreational drugs to help them cope. However, drugs such as alcohol can have a 
depressant effect, so, when they wear off, can make us feel worse. In some cases, 
people may start to consume them in larger doses to maintain the initial sense of 
relief. However, this can lead to dependence and worsen stress. 
6. It is important to note that the ones mentioned here are not the only causes of 
stress. If you or someone you know is struggling with stress, it may be due to any 
one of these factors or it may be something else entirely. 
7. As long-term stress can be damaging to our mental and physical health, it is 
important to know how to reduce stress through daily coping skills. 
8. To understand when stress is causing you a problem, try to connect your feelings 
or physical signs with the pressure you are facing in your life. Remember to look 
out for the physical warnings, such as tense muscles, over tiredness, headaches or 
migraines, as well as emotional signs like irritability and forgetfulness. 
9. Once you notice feelings of stress in your body or mind, try to pause for a 
moment. What happened right before you noticed these feelings? Tracking your 
physical and mental sensations of stress over a couple of days or weeks can help 
you pinpoint where they are coming from and where in your life you could make 
changes. Check your lifestyle habits, engage in physical activity, get restful sleep 
and keep a healthy diet. Be kind to yourself by not blaming yourself and 
practicing regular self-care, maintain a routine with a definite daily pattern of 
sleeping, eating and exercising. Stay away from alcohol and nicotine. 
10. It is okay to ask for professional help if you feel that you are struggling to manage 
stress on your own.   
      (Adapted from Mentalhealth.org.uk) 
 
Activity 2: Discussing the main points. 
 Use the question-and-answer technique to lead a discussion of the new vocabulary    in 
passage  
 
Activity 3: Retelling details of stories or passages.  
 Ask learners to say any facts they have learnt from the passage 
Activity 4: Scanning pieces of texts to locate specific information.  
 Ask learners to answer the questions in their books  
Activity 5: Answering factual and inference questions.  
 Ask learners questions based on the reading passage

6 
 
Activity 6: Deducing meanings of unfamiliar words and idiomatic expressions.  
 Learners bring out unfamiliar words and they are discussed in class 
Activity 7: Drawing inferences from texts by direct or indirect reference.  
 Learners answer questions based on the reading passage. 
Activity 8: Describing feelings, qualities and motives of characters in stories.  
 Ask the learners to express their feelings about the passage 
  
QUESTIONS 
1. According to Paragraph 1, stress is a… 
A. challenge. 
B. change. 
C. demand. 
D. feeling.  
 
2. Paragraph 1, the following are causes of stress in young and older adults except… 
A. becoming parents 
B. employment. 
C. examinations. 
D. health conditions 
 
3. According to the passage, a young trained person awaiting deployment is likely to suffer 
from... 
A. Long term stress 
B. Mental health stress 
C. Stress in relationships 
D. Work related stress 
 
4. Fill in the blanks with one word to complete information from Paragraph 4 
Many (i)………………….. need financial (ii)…………………. to avoid suffering the 
effects of the high (iii) …………………. of living as they struggle to pay                               
(iv) ………………….. and grocery prices.  
5. Paragraph 5. “However, this can lead to dependence…” What does the word ‘this’ refer 
to? 
A. can feel worsened stress 
B. consuming drugs in larger doses 
C. coping with recreational drugs  
D. turning to alcohol and nicotine 
 
6. Which of the following statements is true about the passage? 
 
A. 
Alcohol is a solution to stress

7 
 
B. 
Tense muscles is a physical warning of stress 
C. 
There are very few situations that can cause stress  
D. 
To overcome stress, avoid sleeping and exercising 
 
7. What would be the best title for this passage? 
A. Causes of stress 
B. Challenges and stress 
C. Health and stress 
D. Stress in teenagers 
 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials: 
 Teaching module 
 Worksheets with vocabulary 
 Reading comprehension passage (text on life skills) 
EXPECTED STANDARD- Information read and comprehended correctly. 
 
Assessment:  
 Learners discuss the main points of the passage in pairs.  
 Use the question-and-answer technique to lead a discussion of the new vocabulary 
in the passage.  
 Learners comprehensively answer questions based on the passage 
 
SUMMARY  
 Intensive Reading is the ability to read with understanding.  
 It allows a learner to understand other subjects in school.  
 Learners need to develop skills in reading at a good speed.  
 It is important for learners to apply intensive reading skills in daily life. 
 Comprehension skills help learners to reach deeper meaning in the text. 
 
COMPOSITION WRITING 
TOPIC: NARRATIVES 
1.0 INTRODUCTION 
 Overview: Narrative writing is a type of composition that tells a story with a clear 
beginning, middle and end. It involves presenting a sequence of events, either real or 
imagined, in a way that engages the reader. It usually has characters who drive the action. 
Narratives can be written in different ways with characters as the main movers of the 
action. A strong narrative engages the audience by creating vivid imagery and emotional 
depth. It can be fiction or non-fiction. In this topic we are going to focus on story writing. 
 General Competence(s)

8 
 
- 
Communication: Effectively share one’s thoughts and emotions and 
understand others 
- 
Creativity and Innovation: Organise information and ideas  
 Hook: What if the life you thought was yours was actually a lie?  
 
Key Terms /Words/Vocabulary  
Plot: sequence of events in a story 
Sequence: order in which events are told 
Characters: people or animals that take prat in the events  
Setting: time and space where the story takes place 
Point of view: the perspective from which the story is told 
Theme: the underlying message or lesson of the story  
Narrator: The voice that tells the story 
Suspense: a technique used to build tension and keep the audience 
 
2.0 SUB-TOPIC: Story Writing 
 INTRODUCTION  
Story writing is an important aspect of writing that allows a writer to share experiences and 
feelings and lets readers know about different cultures and ways of life. Story writing or story 
telling enhances creativity and imagination. A story can be told from different points of view. 
This lesson will allow learners to tell stories. 
 SPECIFIC COMPETENCE: Write stories 
LEARNING ACTIVITIES 
Activity 1:  Identifying the elements of a narrative 
 Ask Learners to read a given story (Teacher can also read the story aloud to the class) 
 
 
Sample story 
A Bridge for Nabwalya Village 
In Nabwalya village of the Muchinga escarpment, there was a river that divided 
the land. For many years, the villagers struggled to cross it, using canoes that 
were often swept away by strong currents. 
In the village was a young woman named Mulenga. She was not a village elder 
and she did not have any position of leadership. Mulenga saw the struggles of 
the people and decided to act. One day, after nearly losing her only child to the 
rushing waters, she gathered the villagers and proposed an idea. She suggested 
that the villagers unite to build a sturdy bridge that could connect the two sides 
of the village.

9 
 
Many villagers doubted her. Mulenga’s cousin Bupe even teased her about how 
a helpless woman like her could manage to convince the entire village. 
“We have always lived this way,” said one old woman 
Others wondered how they could build a bridge without any materials. Despite 
the discouragement, Mulenga refused to give up on her idea. She started 
gathering some stones from the river bank and piled them in heaps. She started 
calling on other young women to help her. The women saw her determination 
and slowly one by one they started joining her. Seeing the women’s efforts, the 
village carpenter offered his skills, the blacksmith his tools, and the children 
carried smaller stones.  
Days turned into months, through thick and thin, Mulenga never lost hope. 
When motivation waned, she reminded them of their vision- a future where no 
one feared to cross the river. She listened to their concerns. When new 
challenges came, she adapted to them and ensured that every hand and effort 
felt valued.  
Finally, after a year, the bridge stood strong, a testament to what unity and 
leadership could achieve. On the day of its unveiling, the village celebrated not 
only the crossing, but also the woman who had led them with courage, patience 
and unwavering belief. 
 
 Lead learners in identifying the elements of a story based on the story read 
The following are the elements of the story: 
- 
Setting: place, time, lifestyle, and weather associated with the events in a story 
- 
Character: person or another being in a narrative e.g. main characters and minor 
characters 
- 
Plot: sequence of events in which each event affects the next 
- 
Theme: central topic or message in a story 
- 
Style: a unique way an author expresses their thoughts through word choice 
- 
Conflict: struggle between opposing forces in a narrative 
 Emphasise that a story should have the above discussed elements in order to give clarity 
and capture the reader's interest. 
 
Activity 2. Composing stories 
 Teacher divides learners into groups and distributes worksheets with jumbled 
Sentences (based on picture strips/jumbled sentences, paragraphs...) 
· Teacher asks learners to rearrange them and create a story. 
 
 
Activity 3: Writing short stories using a clear layout  
 Teacher explains that a story should have a beginning, middle and ending 
 Ask Learners to write narratives based on given situations (completing story or writing 
from a question, changing a point of view) 
  Instruct Learners to complete the given story using the first person narration:  
Example:

10 
 
In my village, Nabwalya of the Muchinga escarpment, there was a river that divided the land. 
For many years, we struggled to cross it using canoes that were swept away by strong currents. 
………………………………………………………………………………………………………
………………………………………………………………………………………………………
…………………………………. 
Suggested teaching and learning materials: 
 Plot maps 
 Model story 
 Picture strips showing various settings and characters 
 Worksheets with prompts 
 
EXPECTED STANDARD: Stories written correctly 
Assessment: Ask learners to write stories from a different point of view. 
SUMMARY  
 Story writing involves telling a real or imaginary story  
 When writing short stories, there is need to follow a clear layout (beginning, middle and 
ending) 
 A story needs to have a title, plot, characters, setting, theme, conflict, style (elements of a 
story) 
 It is possible to write stories based on given situations (completing story or writing from 
a question,  
 
STRUCTURE 
Topic: RESULT 
1. 0 INTRODUCTION  
 Overview: Result is used to express the outcome or effect of a particular cause or action. 
The structure helps us to connect actions to their consequences.  
 General Competence(s) 
- 
Communication: Express oneself (spoken and written) clearly and effectively 
- 
Use appropriate language in different situations 
 Hook: Every action we take leads to an outcome. What language can help us to express 
how one event triggers another?

11 
 
KEY TERMS /WORDS/VOCABULARY  
 Cause and effect: the relationship between an action (cause) and its consequence (effect) 
 Outcome: The end result or effect of an action 
 Consequence: a result, especially one that follows an action naturally or logically 
2.0. SUBTOPIC: Different Ways of Expressing Result 
 INTRODUCTION 
When we want to express result in a sentence, we use certain structures to show cause 
and effect relationships. In such constructions, an action or situation is connected to 
its result. This lesson explores different ways of expressing result. 
3.0.SPECIFIC COMPETENCE: Use different expressions of result in sentences 
4.0.LEARNING ACTIVITIES  
Activity 1: Discussing different expressions of result 
Type 1: Using Therefore, as a result, for this reason, consequently, to express result 
 Ask a tall learner to come to the board and touch the top of the board.  
 Then write:  
 
 
 
 
 
Lombe is tall. Therefore, she can touch the top of the board. 
 
 Explain that in this type of expression of result, two sentences are used. 
 
 
 
e.g.  The bridge was swept away. Therefore, Mwila could not cross the river. 
                                                        As a result, 
                                                        For this reason, 
                                                        Consequently, 
Note that a comma is needed after the word used to express result. 
 Explain the other ways of expressing result as follows: 
Type 2: Using so to express result 
The two ideas are joined into one sentence using the word so 
A comma is needed before the word so. 
 
 
 
               e.g.    Lombe is tall, so she can touch the top of the board 
 
 
The bridge was swept away, so Mwila could not cross the river. 
 
 
She learnt how to budget, so she saved enough money to buy a new bag.

12 
 
Type 3:   Using so…that to express result  
In this type, the two ideas are joined by using so…that 
Between so and that, there should be an adjective or an adverb. 
 
e.g.      Lombe is so tall that she can touch the top of the board 
 
 
Annel was so careful that he did not contract Covid-19. 
 
Type 4: Using such (a)…that to express result 
In this type, such (a)…that is used to join two ideas into one sentence. The article a is used 
when referring to a singular noun and is dropped when referring to a plural noun. 
 
e.g.  Lombe is such a tall girl that she can touch the top of the board 
Activity 2: Constructing sentences to express Result  
 Ask learners to look around the classroom and construct sentences to express result. 
e.g.  
The classroom is so big that we can all fit. 
The floor is very clean. Therefore, I can sit on it. 
The room is so dark that I cannot see. 
 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials: 
 Table showing expressions of cause and effect 
 Chart 
 Flashcards with sentences expressing result 
 
EXPECTED STANDARD: Different expressions of result in sentences used correctly 
Assessment  
Ask learners to pick sentences from column A and join with one from column B. Use any of the 
words learnt to express result. 
A 
B 
The boys were found using drugs 
Our ice cream melted 
There was too much Gender based violence in 
the country 
She collapsed at the office 
Mazuba was a good leader 
She could not buy a ticket for the movie 
The accountant did not have enough rest 
Everyone liked her 
Mulimona did not have breakfast 
The women staged a demonstration 
Bupe finished her pocket money 
They were arrested by the Drug Enforcement 
Commission

13 
 
It was very hot 
She felt weak 
 
SUMMARY  
 Discussing different expressions of result 
 Constructing sentences to express Result  
TOPIC: SUMMARY WRITING 
1.0. INTRODUCTION  
 Overview: A summary is a concise and brief retelling of the main points and key details 
from a larger piece of content, such as an article, book, movie or speech. It extracts the 
essential information into a shorter format while preserving the core message or 
storyline. Summarising is a vital skill in both academic and everyday settings. It allows 
individuals to shorten lengthy texts or stories into their most important points, making 
information more manageable and easier to communicate.  
       This topic will look at Title Summary. 
 General Competence(s):  
- 
Critical Thinking: Analyse texts to improve the ability to understand.  
- 
Creativity and Innovation: Organise information and ideas  
 
 Hook: What if the success of a story or idea depended on just one perfect line-The title?  
 KEY TERMS /WORDS/VOCABULARY  
 Title- the name of a book, composition, or other artistic work.  
          A name or label given to something 
 
2.0. SUB-TOPIC - TITLE SUMMARY 
 INTRODUCTION:  
A concise title can help convey the main idea of the passage and provide context for the 
reader. Titles are like a sneak peek into the content they represent, giving us a glimpse of 
what to expect, and always appear at the top of the passage or cover of a book.

14 
 
 
Specific Competence(s) – Choose best title summary 
 
Learning Activities  
Activity 1: Reading given passage. 
 Ask learners to read a given passage. 
 
Too many challenges in one’s life affect mental health. Your overall well-
being, how you think, feel and handle life can show the state of your mental 
health. Mental health care should be a priority in anyone’s life.  It is all about, 
consistent efforts that strengthen resilience and help you feel at ease. Making 
self-care a priority, staying active, connecting with other people helps you to 
improve your mental health. Setting realistic goals for yourself and seeking 
professional help when you need it can also help. 
A. Causes of Mental Health 
B. Improving Mental Health 
C. Mental Health Care 
D. Mental Health Challenge 
 
  
Fast food may be useful, but its regular consumption can have serious health 
concerns. Many fast foods are high in unhealthy fats, sugars and sodium which 
can contribute to obesity, heart disease, diabetes and high blood pressure. Fast 
foods can sometimes lack important nutrients, resulting in poor food balance. 
Fast foods have environmental and economic disadvantages. They increase 
waste and cause dependence on large companies that may be more interested 
in making profit than healthy food. While a special fast-food meal once in a 
while is not harmful, careful choices are necessary to maintain a healthy 
lifestyle. 
A. Consumption of Fast Foods 
B. Convenient Fast Foods 
C. Moderation of Fast Foods 
D. Negative Effects of Fast Foods 
 
Choosing best title for given paragraph 
 Ask the learners to recall what they learnt in the first term on titles  
 Explain that a title is a summary of what a passage, book, film, song is about. It is a 
name or label given to something.

15 
 
 The learners are led into discussing a title and its characteristics 
 
Characteristics of a good title  
Clear- straight forward and easy to understand, avoiding ambiguity. 
Brief –concise delivering meaning in as few words as possible 
Relevant- connects with the content or subject it represents. It grabs attention and set 
expectations. 
 
 Ask learners to choose the best title from the given passages 
Supplying the best title summary.  
 Provide a passage to the learners and ask them to give it a title. 
Example: 
A good life is every person’s dream. Every human being wants to 
enjoy a healthy and happy life. One way of achieving this is 
through personal hygiene. Personal hygiene is a practice of 
maintaining cleanliness and grooming of the body to promote 
health and wellbeing. However, some people do not realise the 
importance of hygiene. For instance, Mutinta, a Form one pupil 
once struggled with frequent illnesses and low self-esteem. He 
rarely bathed, wore dirty clothes, and never brushed his teeth. 
His classmates avoided him, and he felt lonely and sad. One day, 
his teacher gave a lesson on personal hygiene and its importance

his teeth twice a day, and washing his clothes regularly. 
Suggested teaching and learning materials: 
 Sample paragraphs 
EXPECTED STANDARD- Best title summary chosen correctly.  
Assessment 
Ask learners to read given passages and provide suitable titles.  
   
 
SUMMARY  
 A good title gives a clear idea of what a piece of writing contains, yet it uses very 
few words.  
 It is supposed to be clear, brief and relevant.

CHAPTER 2: NATIONAL VALUES AND PRINCIPLES 
LISTENING AND SPEAKING 
DRAMA 
1.0.  INTRODUCTION  
 Overview: Drama is a type of storytelling performed on stage or screen, where actors 
portray characters and emotions through dialogue and actions, often exploring conflicts 
and themes in human life. Drama, as an art form, has been a powerful medium for 
storytelling for centuries, and it continues to be relevant in various aspects of society. 
This topic will explore short plays, a form of drama that tells interesting stories within a 
limited timeframe. It will examine their structure, key elements, and how they reflect 
real-life issues. Short plays offer a condensed, yet impactful way to engage with complex 
human emotions and situations, making them relevant in both entertainment and life 
lessons.  
 General Competence(s):  
- 
Communication: 
 Effectively share one’s thoughts and emotions and understand others. 
 Manage one’s emotions. 
- 
Creativity and innovation: 
 Organise information and ideas 
 Justify one’s reasoning and procedures 
 Ask thoughtful questions 
 Hook:  Can someone make you laugh, cry, or rethink your own beliefs?  
 KEY TERMS /WORDS/VOCABULARY:  
 Short Play: A concise form of drama typically lasting 15-30 minutes, focusing on 
brief yet impactful storytelling. 
 Character: The individuals in the play who perform actions, speak dialogue, and 
undergo development throughout the story.  
 Characterisation: The way in which an author develops characters, showing their 
personality and growth throughout the play. 
 Audience: The people who watch the performance, interpret the play, and respond to 
its themes, emotions, and events.

17 
 
 Narrator: (If present) A voice or character that provides background information or 
commentary to help the audience understand the context or events. 
 Scene: A specific section of the play, often occurring in a single location, where 
characters interact and events unfold. Scenes typically change as the setting or time 
shifts. 
 Act: A major division in the play, often consisting of several scenes. Acts help 
structure the flow of the story and mark significant changes in the plot. 
 Plot: The sequence of events in the play, including the conflict, climax, and 
resolution, that drive the action and reveal the themes. 
 Dialogue: The conversation between characters in a play, which drives the plot and 
reveals character traits. 
 Conflict: The central problem or challenge that drives the plot in a play, often 
between characters or within oneself. 
 Resolution: The conclusion or solution to the conflict, often providing closure to the 
story. 
 Setting: The time and place in which the play’s events occur, establishing the context 
for the story. 
 Theme: The underlying message or central idea explored in the play, such as love, 
loss, or justice. 
 
2.0. SUB-TOPIC: SHORT PLAYS 
 Introduction:  
A short play is a form of drama that tells interesting stories within a limited timeframe.  
 Specific Competence: Use appropriate language to dramatise short plays 
Learning Activities: 
Activity 1: Discussing basic elements of a short play 
 Lead Learners in a discussion on the elements of a short play (character, dialogue, 
audience, narrator, scene, act, plot…) 
 Provide the class with a short play excerpt from a simplified version of a famous 
play, Romeo and Juliet by William Shakespeare, or any modern short play that is 
age-appropriate and hand out the first scene.  
 
Step 1: Reading the Excerpt 
Assign learners to read the short play excerpt in groups or pairs. 
Here is a short excerpt from Romeo and Juliet by William Shakespeare, Act 1, Scene 5, 
which you can use for your classroom situation: 
 
Romeo: 
(To Juliet) 
If I'm being too bold by touching your hand,

18 
 
Then let me make it right— 
My lips are like shy pilgrims, 
Ready to smooth things over with a gentle kiss. 
Juliet: 
Good pilgrim, you’re being too hard on your hand— 
It’s showing proper devotion. 
Even saints allow pilgrims to touch their hands— 
Holding palms is like a holy kiss between them. 
Romeo: 
Don’t saints and pilgrims have lips too? 
Juliet: 
Yes, but lips are meant for praying. 
Romeo: 
Then, my dear saint, let lips do what hands do: 
They pray. Please let me kiss you— 
Or my faith might turn into despair. 
Juliet: 
Saints don’t move, even when prayers are granted. 
So don’t move—let me answer your prayer this way. 
(She kisses him) 
Now, by your kiss, you’ve taken away my sin. 
Romeo: 
Then my lips now carry that sin. What should I do? 
(They kiss again) 
This excerpt is from the famous moment when Romeo and Juliet meet and share their first kiss. 
It’s a great scene to analyse, as it contains key elements of character, dialogue, and plot. 
 
Step 2: Group Discussion  
After learners have read the scene, divide the class into small groups. Each group is given 
a set of questions to help them focus on identifying the basic elements of the play. 
Questions could include: 
 
Character: Who are the main characters in this scene? What do we learn about them 
through their dialogue and actions? 
 
Dialogue: What important information is revealed through the characters’ 
conversations? How does the dialogue express the emotions or conflict in the scene? 
 
Audience: Imagine you are watching this play performed. What kind of reactions 
might the audience have to this scene? 
 
Narrator: Is there a narrator in this scene? If not, how does the dialogue or the stage 
direction help us understand the story?

19 
 
 
Scene: What is the setting of the story? How does it affect the mood or tone of the 
play? 
 
Act: How does this scene fit within the larger structure of the play? If the play were 
divided into acts, what would this scene represent? 
 
Plot: What is the main event or conflict that happens in this scene? How does it move 
the story forward? 
 
Step 3: Whole-Class Sharing 
After the group discussions, ask group representatives to share their thoughts on each of 
the elements. Guide the discussion with additional questions or clarifications. For 
example: 
 
What role does the setting play in shaping the characters’ actions? 
 
How does the dialogue between Romeo and Juliet reveal their feelings toward each 
other? 
 
What would the audience likely feel at this point in the play? 
 
Step 4: Reflection and Application 
 
Ask learners to reflect on the importance of these elements in understanding the play. 
For instance: 
- 
Why do you think the characters’ dialogue is so crucial in revealing the plot of the 
play? 
- 
How do these elements (character, plot, etc.) work together to create the overall 
impact of the play? 
 Ensure that by the end of the lesson, learners have a clearer understanding of how 
basic elements like character, dialogue, and plot contribute to the structure and 
meaning of a short play. 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials: 
 Excerpt from Romeo and Juliet Act 1 scene 5 
 Short plays 
 Character Charts: with names of characters in the play and brief descriptions of 
their traits.  
EXPECTED STANDARD: Language to dramatise short plays used appropriately 
Assessment:  
Written Assessment (Quiz/Tests): 
 Evaluate learners’ understanding of the basic elements of the play through 
individual written responses by administering a written quiz or a set of open-
ended questions. These may include: 
- 
Closed Questions:  
Multiple-choice or true/false questions about the characters, plot, or setting.

20 
 
Example: “Who says the lines, ‘My lips, two blushing pilgrims, ready 
stand…’?” 
- 
Open-Ended Questions:  
Questions that require learners to explain concepts in depth. 
Example: "How does the dialogue between Romeo and Juliet in this scene 
demonstrate their developing relationship?" 
SUMMARY:  
 The topic focuses on analysing the basic elements of a short play using Romeo 
and Juliet. Key elements include character, dialogue, audience, narrator, 
scene, act, and plot, which together shape the structure and meaning of the play. 
 The goal is for learners to understand how these elements interact and contribute 
to the narrative. 
 Learners will analyse these elements to deepen their understanding of any play. 
COMPREHENSION  
1.0 INTRODUCTION  
 Overview: Comprehension is an active process of understanding spoken or written 
information. It is important for real-life tasks, like following instructions, having 
conversations, and interpreting information. This topic focuses on building basic 
comprehension skills, especially listening. Learners will practise listening to 
passages, identifying main ideas, and participating in discussions to improve their 
understanding. 
 General Competence(s):  
- 
Collaboration:  
 Respect the views of group members while working together. 
 Listen to other group members and make meaningful contribution to a given 
task   
- 
Communication:  
 Apply good Listening and Speaking Skills  
 Hook: Have you ever struggled to understand instructions because you missed a key 
detail?

21 
 
KEY TERMS/WORDS/VOCABULARY:   
 Comprehension: The ability to understand something.  
 Passage: A complete piece of writing that conveys a specific idea, message or story. 
 Retelling: Tell a story again or differently. 
 Interpretation: The process of explaining the meaning of something. 
 Main Idea: The primary message, point or argument that a text, passage or author is 
trying to convey.  
 Details: Elements or pieces of information that collectively describe or explain a topic, 
situation or object. 
 Active Listening: Fully concentrating, understanding, and responding to what is being 
said. 
 Discussion: The action or process of talking about something in order to reach a decision 
or to exchange ideas. 
 Paraphrasing: Restating someone else’s ideas in your own words to ensure 
understanding. 
 Listening Skills: The abilities needed to hear, interpret, and respond to spoken 
information. 
 Verbal Communication: The use of words to convey information or express ideas. 
2.0. SUB-TOPIC: LISTENING COMPREHENSION  
 Introduction: Listening comprehension is the ability to understand spoken information. 
In this lesson, learners will practise listening carefully to passages and identifying key 
ideas. This skill is essential for following instructions, engaging in conversations, and 
acquiring new knowledge. 
 Specific Competence(s): Listen and comprehend information.  
Learning Activities:   
Activity 1: Listening to a read passage or information 
 
Write the title “The Tortoise and the Hare” and ask learners to predict what the 
story might be about. 
 
Read the passage and encourage learners to listen carefully. 
 
Explain to the learners that this story teaches valuable lessons about patience, 
perseverance, and the idea that slow and steady can win the race. It is widely

22 
 
recognised across many African cultures, making it a relatable and engaging choice 
for learners. 
The Tortoise and the Hare (Summarised Version) 
Once upon a time, in a forest, there was a boastful hare who was very proud of his speed. 
He often teased the tortoise for being so slow. One day, the tortoise became tired of the 
hare’s teasing and challenged him to a race. 
The hare, confident he would win easily, agreed to the race. When the race began, the 
hare quickly sped ahead and, feeling so sure of his victory, decided to rest under a tree. 
Meanwhile, the tortoise continued to move slowly but steadily, never stopping. 
While the hare slept, the tortoise kept going and eventually passed him. When the hare 
woke up, he rushed to the finish line but was too late. The tortoise had already won the 
race. 
(Source: Aesop’s Fables – “The Tortoise and Hare”) 
Now read the next passage. 
The Lion and the Mouse (Summarised Version) 
One day, a lion was sleeping in the forest when a small mouse accidentally ran across his paw. 
The lion woke up and caught the mouse in his huge paw, ready to eat it. The mouse, frightened, 
begged the lion to let him go, promising to help the lion someday in return. 
The lion, amused by the tiny mouse’s promise, decided to let him go. Not long after, the lion was 
trapped in a hunter’s net. The mouse heard the lion’s roars for help and quickly ran to him. Using 
its sharp teeth, the mouse chewed through the ropes and freed the lion. 
The lion was grateful and realised that even a small creature can be a big help. 
 The story teaches that kindness is never wasted, and even the smallest acts can make a 
big difference.  
Activity 2: Discussing the Passages 
• Pair learners and ask them to re-tell the story in their own words.  
• Ask a few pairs to present to the rest of the class.  
Activity 3: Completing given table while passage is being read.  
 Provide learners with a simple table to fill out as they listen to the passage for the second 
time. 
 Learners should fill in the table as teacher is reading.

23 
 
 
Sample Table: 
Story Element 
Details 
Main Characters Write down the names of the characters in the story 
Setting 
Where and when does the story take place?

What problem do the characters face in the story?

Lesson/Message 
What lesson or moral does the story teach? 
 
Activity 4: Answering questions based on the read passage or information.  
 Ask questions based on the passage. Example questions:   
Questions based on “The Tortoise and the Hare” story: 
1. What made the tortoise win the race despite being slower than the hare? 
2. What lesson do you think the hare learned after losing the race to the tortoise? 
NOTE: These questions will help learners recall specific details from the story while also 
encouraging them to reflect on the underlying message of the tale. 
Ask questions based on “The Lion and the Mouse” passage (These questions will help 
learners engage with the story on both a factual and reflective level):  
1. What do you think the lion learned from his experience with the mouse?

Suggested Teaching and Learning materials:

 Pre-recorded stories/passages 
EXPECTED STANDARD: Information listened to and comprehended correctly.  
Assessment:

 Written Assessment: Observe how learners participate in the given assignment and 
whether they are able to listen to information and comprehend correctly.

24 
 
SUMMARY:  
 Comprehension is the ability to understand spoken or written information. 
 Listening carefully is the first step to good comprehension. 
 Activities like re-telling a story, filling in tables, and answering questions improve

 These activities help learners focus on key details and better process the information. 
 Engaging in these activities promotes better retention and interpretation of what has been 
heard or read.

Topic: DESCRIPTIVE WRITING 
1.0. INTRODUCTION  
 Overview: Descriptive writing is an essential skill in both personal and professional 
contexts, as it allows individuals to vividly convey experiences, emotions, and 
observations to others. In real life, effective descriptive writing helps people 
communicate more clearly and persuasively, whether it is in creative writing, 
business reports, or everyday conversations. Descriptive writing plays a vital role in

We can write a description of a person, an animal or an object. 
This lesson will look at a description of an animal.

KEY TERMS /WORDS/VOCABULARY

 Figurative 
Language: 
Non-literal 
expressions 
like

2.0. SUB-TOPIC: Describing an Animal 
 Introduction: Descriptive composition involves painting a vivid picture of a subject 
using detailed language. When describing an animal, the aim is to capture its 
appearance, behaviour, and unique traits to engage the reader's senses. The main 
focus is on describing an animal's physical features, movements, sounds, and habitat.

2.1.SPECIFIC COMPETENCE: Write a description of an animal.    
Learning Activities: 
Activity 1: Reading model descriptive compositions on animals.  
 Learners read the passage. 
A Description of a Monkey  
A monkey is a lively creature, full of energy. Its fur is a soft blanket of golden-brown, 
with a lighter cream colour on its belly. Like a crown, its fur glows in the sunlight, 
shimmering as it moves. Its face is full of character: large, dark eyes that sparkle with 
curiosity, a small, flat nose, and a mischievous smile that never fades. 
The monkey’s hands are like tiny, delicate claws, quick and nimble. It swings through the 
trees with the grace of a dancer, leaping from branch to branch, its long tail curling like 
a coiled rope to keep balance. With each leap, its tail snaps and sways, a silent partner to 
its acrobatics. 
The forest is filled with the monkey’s chatter—high-pitched calls that rise and fall like a 
song. "Chatter-chatter!" it cries, the sound echoing off the trees. When it moves, it is a 
blur of motion, quick as lightning, darting in every direction. Its tiny feet tap, tap, tap on 
the tree trunks, a rhythmic beat against the jungle's hum. 
It pauses, eyes wide with excitement, and plucks a juicy fruit from a branch. Its small 
hands twist it free, bringing it to its lips with the precision of a magician. The fruit 
crunches as the monkey bites, the sound sharp and satisfying in the quiet air.

26 
 
In the thick, green jungle, the monkey is at home. The trees stretch high above, their 
leaves rustling like whispers in the wind. The monkey moves through this world, alive and 
ever-curious, with the forest as its playground and the sky as its limit.

Summary

STRUCTURE 1 
TOPIC: ADVERBS 
1.0.Introduction 
 
Overview: Adverbs are words that modify or give more information about verbs, 
adjectives, or other adverbs in a sentence. Understanding the position of adverbs is 
crucial in constructing clear and accurate sentences. This topic will explore the different 
positions where adverbs can appear within a sentence, such as before or after the main 
verb, at the beginning or end of a sentence, and how these positions can change the 
meaning or emphasis of the sentence.  
 
General Competence: 
- 
Communication: Express oneself (Spoken and written) clearly and 
effectively. 
 
 
Hook: What is the difference between these two sentences? Only Sara ate the cake. 
Versus Sara only ate the cake.  
 KEY TERMS / WORDS / VOCABULARY 
 
Adverb: A word that modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb. 
 
Verb: An action or state of being in a sentence. 
 
Adjective: A word that modifies or describes a noun. 
 
Adverbial Phrase: A group of words that act as an adverb. 
 
Position: The placement of an adverb within a sentence (e.g., beginning, middle, end). 
 
Modifier: A word or phrase that provides more information about another element in the 
sentence. 
 
Emphasis: The focus or importance given to a particular part of the sentence, often 
influenced by adverb placement.

28 
 
2.0. SUBTOPIC: POSITION OF ADVERBS 
 
Introduction: 
Adverbs modify verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs, and their position plays a key role in 
sentence structure and meaning. By understanding the placement of different types of 
adverbs (such as those of place, time, frequency, manner, and many others), learners will 
gain a deeper understanding of sentence construction, improving both their written and 
spoken communication. In this topic, learners will explore how adverbs are positioned 
within sentences and how their placement can affect the overall meaning and emphasis of 
the sentence. 
 
 
Specific Competence: Use adverbs in different positions in sentences. 
 
 Learning Activities: 
Activity 1: Discussing the Different Kinds of Adverbs  
 Introduce and explain the different kinds of adverbs: adverbs of place, time, 
frequency, manner, and degree. 
- 
Adverbs of place answer the question "Where?" (e.g., here, there, 
everywhere). 
- 
Adverbs of time answer the question "When?" (e.g., now, later, 
soon). 
- 
Adverbs of frequency answer the question "How often?" (e.g., 
always, often, never). 
- 
Adverbs of manner answer the question "How?" (e.g., quickly, 
carefully, slowly). 
- 
Adverbs of degree answer the question "To what extent?" (e.g., very, 
too, quite). 
 
Give class a set of sentences with adverbs missing, as an example. 
Example:  
1. Peter ate the fruit _________, (hungrily) 
2. I _______read a book before going to bed. (always) 
3. The bag was _____heavy _________.(very) 
4. left the book __________. (here 
5. She painted the portrait _______.(beautifully) 
6. The song was ______composed. (creatively) 
 
Learners work in pairs or small groups to identify the type of adverb that 
would fit into each sentence and explain why. 
 
Learners then share their answers and reasoning with the class. 
 
Clarify any misunderstandings.

29 
 
Activity 2: Using Adverbs in Different Positions in Sentences 
 Present a series of sentences with adverbs in different positions (e.g., beginning, 
middle, and end of the sentence). 
 Class analyses how the meaning of the sentence changes based on the placement 
of the adverb. For example: 
 
There they are. vs. They are there. 
 
She spoke softly. vs. Softly, she spoke. 
 Learners work individually or in pairs to re-arrange sentences with given adverbs, 
focusing on the placement of the adverb and discussing the effects of changing its 
position. 
 Explain that: 
- 
adverbs of place typically follow the verb or object, though they can be moved to 
the beginning of a sentence for emphasis (e.g., She went there vs. There she 
went). 
- 
Adverbs of time can be placed at the beginning or end of a sentence, but placing 
them at the beginning emphasizes the time element (e.g., Tomorrow, we will 
leave vs. We will leave tomorrow). 
- 
Adverbs of frequency usually come before the main verb (e.g., "She always 
wakes up early) but after the verb "to be" (e.g., She is always late). 
- 
Adverbs of manner often follow the verb or object, but can also be placed at the 
beginning of the sentence for stylistic effect (e.g., He runs fast vs. Fast, he runs.) 
- 
Changing the position of an adverb can change the emphasis or focus of the 
sentence. 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials: 
 Sentence strips with incomplete sentences for learners to complete with adverbs. 
 Black/Whiteboard for demonstrating how word order changes in a sentence. 
 Handouts with sentences for learners to rearrange and discuss. 
4. Expected Standard: Adverbs in different positions in sentences used appropriately. 
Assessment: 
 
Observation: Teacher observes learners as they classify adverbs and 
provide examples, checking for understanding of each adverb's type 
(place, time, frequency, manner, degree). 
 
Questioning: Ask learners to give additional examples of each type of 
adverb and explain their function in the sentence. This will help gauge 
whether they can identify adverbs correctly.

30 
 
 
Learner Review: During group work, learners can review each other’s 
classifications of adverbs to promote collaborative learning and correct 
any misunderstandings. 
 
Quiz on Adverbs: Make sure learners understand the multiple-choice and 
fill-in-the-blank questions are based on the lesson's content. 
Summary 
 
Adverbs are words that modify verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs in a sentence. 
 
There are different types of adverbs namely Adverbs of place, Adverbs of time, Adverbs 
of frequency, Adverbs of manner and Adverbs of degree  
 
Adverbs can appear at different positions in a sentence. Changing the position of an 
adverb affects the meaning, tone, or emphasis of the sentence. 
 
Learning to use adverbs correctly in sentences is crucial for effective communication and 
enhancing sentence variety. 
STRUCTURE 2 
TOPIC: ADJECTIVES 
1.0.INTRODUCTION 
 Overview: Adjectives are words used to describe or modify nouns and pronouns. They 
help add detail, emotion and colour to language, making speech and writing more vivid 
and precise. Adjectives answer questions like what kind, how many, and which one.

 General Competence(s):

Communication:

KEY TERMS/WORDS/ VOCABULARY 
 Adjective: a word that describes or says more about a noun. 
 Order: arrangement of people or things in relation to each other according to a 
particular sequence, pattern or method. 
 Noun: a word that denotes a person, place or thing. 
 Pronoun: a word that takes the place of a noun.

2.0. SUB-TOPIC: ORDER OF ADJECTIVES

STRUCTURE 3 
TOPIC: INTENSIFIERS 
1.0. INTRODUCTION

33 
 
 Overview: An intensifier is a word that strengthens or weakens or give emphasis to 
another word. The purpose of an intensifier is to tell us the intensity of another word.  
 General Competence(s): 
- 
Communication:  
 Use appropriate language (verbal and non-verbal) in different situations. 
 Express oneself (spoken and written) clearly and effectively. 
 Hook: Imagine you are asked to describe the best day of your life? 
KEY TERMS/ WORDS/ VOCABULARY 
 Degree: extent to which something happens. 
 Intensity: strength, magnitude or degree of something. 
 Adverb: a word that modifies a verb, an adjective or another adverb. 
 intensifier: a word (often an adverb) used to strengthen or weaken the meaning of 
another word. 
 Modifier: a word, phrase or clause that qualifies or describes another word. 
2.0. SUBTOPIC: Using Intensifiers 
 Intensifiers are words that can add intensity, degree or emphasis to a statement. There are 
several types of intensifiers. Some of them include: very, much, too, fairly and rather, 
quite, hardly, barely and scarcely, almost and nearly, just. 
  
 
 Specific Competences: Use intensifiers in sentences 
3.0. Learning Activities: 
Activity 1: Discussing the use of intensifiers  
 Give learners three sentences which have intensifiers 
Example:  
1. Joyce is incredibly smart 
2. Chilombu is extremely happy. 
3. This meal is absolutely delicious 
4. I am utterly exhausted. 
 Underline the intensifiers in the sentences  
 Ask the learners what role the words are doing in the sentences. 
 Explain intensifiers (adverbs) which strengthen, or give emphasis to other words. 
Intensifiers  
i. 
Very means to a high degree  
He is a very strong man.

34 
 
This pie is very tasty.  
ii. 
Too means more than is necessary 
 
 
This tea is too hot to drink. 
  
 
I am too cold to walk in the rain. 
What is wrong with these sentences? 
 
 
He is too rich. 
                        Siaciwena is too intelligent. 
                       The party was too enjoyable. 
These sentences do not make sense. For them to make sense we have to replace too with very. 
He is very rich. 
Siaciwena is very intelligent. 
You should not use too when you mean very.            
iii. 
Much means a large amount or to a large degree. 
                      I feel much better. 
                      I don’t earn much money 
iv. 
Fairly and rather both mean ‘a little or moderately’. However, fairly suggests a 
more positive attitude than rather. 
 
      Kisu is fairly tall. 
      I am fairly good at mathematics. 
      Vitumbiko is rather lazy. 
v. 
Quite has two different uses. It can mean completely or a little less than. 
My answer is quite different from yours. (completely). 
Your composition is quite good. (a little less). 
vi. 
Hardly/barely/scarcely are near negatives and mean almost not. 
He hardly ever goes to church. (almost doesn’t)                
We hardly had time to catch the train. (almost no time). 
I was so tired I could scarcely keep my eyes open. (almost could not) 
vii. 
Almost and nearly are used to mean very near but not quite there. 
The match had almost finished when the captain scored. 
viii. 
Just can have a similar meaning to barely, hardly and scarcely. 
The party was just beginning when it started to rain. 
He just managed to buy a car. 
Just is often used with only. 
                We only just finished the exercise.                                        
Activity 2: Identifying intensifiers in sentences 
 Provide more sentences to the learners and ask them to identify words that are 
strengthening, or giving emphasis to other words.

35 
 
Example: 
1. The examination was fairly easy. 
2. This food is very cold. 
3. Musonda is too conservative. 
4. Kalenga is quite shy.

Summary 
 Use intensifiers sparingly to avoid over emphasising or sounding insincere. 
 Choose the right intensifier to convey the intended degree of emphasis. 
 By using intensifiers, you can add appropriate depth and emphasis to your language 
making your communication more engaging.

TOPIC: INTRODUCTION TO SUMMARY 
1.0 
Introduction  
- 
Overview: A summary is a concise and clear restatement of the main ideas or 
points from a larger piece of text. It distils essential information while omitting 
unnecessary details, allowing readers to grasp the core meaning in a shorter form. 
Summarising can be done in various ways, and one of the most common methods 
is through a sentence summary. 
This topic will look at Sentence Summary. 
- 
General Competence(s):  
- 
Critical Thinking:  
 Analyse texts to improve the ability to understand.  
- 
Creativity and Innovation:  
 Organise information and ideas  
- 
Hook: Have you ever explained a long story to someone in a few words but still 
kept the full meaning? 
KEY TERMS/WORDS/VOCABULARY   
 
Summary: A brief and clear restatement of the main points or ideas of a text. 
 
Main Idea: The central concept or argument in a piece of writing. 
 
Concise: Expressing much in few words; brief but clear. 
 
Condense: To make something shorter by removing unnecessary details. 
 
Essential Information: The most important and relevant details. 
 
Overview: A general summary or outline of the content. 
 
Key Points: The most important aspects or elements of the material. 
 
Restate: To express the same idea in different words, usually more briefly. 
 
Omit: To leave out or exclude. 
 
Snapshot: A quick and clear representation of something, often capturing the main 
aspects in a short form.

37 
 
3.0. SUB-TOPIC: SENTENCE SUMMARY  
 Introduction: A sentence summary condenses the original material into a single, concise 
sentence that communicates the main idea or purpose of the content. It serves as a brief 
overview, providing a snapshot of the content in just a few words or a single sentence, 
while still capturing the essence of the original material. When writing a sentence 
summary, the goal is to highlight the most important points and ideas without getting 
bogged down in excessive detail. This practice is useful for quickly understanding and 
recalling key concepts. 
Specific Competence(s): Choose the best sentence summary.  
Learning Activities   
Activity 1: Reading the Given Summary Passage (Paragraph) 
 Provide a paragraph to the learners for reading. 
Passage 
In recent years, the global community has witnessed an increasing shift 
toward renewable energy sources. As the harmful effects of fossil fuels 
become more evident, countries are looking for sustainable alternatives. 
Solar power, wind energy, and hydropower are leading the way, with 
advancements in technology making these sources more efficient and cost-
effective. Governments around the world are investing in green energy 
projects, and many companies are beginning to transition to more eco-
friendly practices. This shift not only helps in reducing greenhouse gas 
emissions but also promotes energy security and long-term economic 
growth. 
Despite the progress, challenges remain in the global transition to 
renewable energy. Some regions still lack the infrastructure or investment 
needed to adopt these technologies widely. Additionally, the intermittent 
nature of sources like solar and wind requires improved energy storage 
solutions. However, with continued innovation, international cooperation, 
and public support, the world can move closer to a cleaner, more 
sustainable energy future.

38 
 
Activity 2: choosing the best sentence summary for a given paragraph 
 
Ask learners to read the paragraph carefully. 
 
Encourage the class to discuss the main points of the passage.  
 
Use guiding questions to ensure the learners grasp the essential concepts. 
 
Ask learners to choose the best sentence summary from the following: 
1. Countries are replacing fossil fuels with renewable energy because it’s cheaper 
and easier to use. 
2. Solar and wind energy are the only alternatives being used to replace fossil fuels 
globally. 
3. The global focus on renewable energy is mainly about saving money and avoiding 
fossil fuel shortages. 
4. The world is shifting to renewable energy as a cleaner, sustainable alternative to

Activity 3: Supplying the best Sentence Summary 
 
Ask learners to condense paragraph two into a single, clear sentence that summarises 
the core message. 
 
Guidance for Sentence Summary: 
 
Explain that the goal is to capture the main idea of the paragraph in one sentence. 
 
Focus on the key points: the shift to renewable energy, the sources mentioned (solar, 
wind, hydropower), and the benefits. 
Example of a Suitable Sentence Summary:  
"Due to the harmful effects of fossil fuels, there has been a global shift toward renewable 
energy sources like solar, wind, and hydropower, which are more efficient and contribute to 
reducing emissions, improving energy security, and promoting economic growth." 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials: 
 Summary passage 
 Sample sentence summary 
2.1 
EXPECTED STANDARD: Best sentence summary chosen correctly  
Assessment:   
 
Ask learners to pair up and share their sentence summaries with each other.

39 
 
 
Encourage learners to give constructive feedback on how well the sentence 
captures the core message of the paragraph. 
 
Encourage learners to practise writing sentence summaries for other passages as a 
way of improving their comprehension and synthesis skills. 
Home work:  
 Give worksheets with paragraph texts and ask learners to read and make sentence 
summaries.  
 Summary  
 Summary writing is important because it enhances comprehension skills and 
helps learners to improve writing skills by demonstrating the ability to absorb 
information.  
 Summary writing also helps learners use the skill of Summarising to keep notes 
that would help them remember what they have read. 
 Reiterate the importance of sentence summaries in understanding the core 
meaning of larger texts.

2.0. SUB-TOPIC: EXTENSIVE READING 
 Introduction:  
Extensive reading can be defined as reading done for pleasure or gaining general 
understanding by focusing on a large volume of materials. The goal of extensive reading 
is to expose learners to a wide range of texts, vocabulary, and sentence structures in a 
natural context helping to improve reading fluency, language learning, and overall

 Ask learners to select books of choice from a collection and read  
Understanding HIV and AIDS 
Extensive reading on HIV and AIDS opens the gateway to knowledge, 
empathy, and informed action. HIV, or Human Immunodeficiency Virus, 
is a virus that attacks the immune system, and if untreated, can lead to 
AIDS (Acquired Immune deficiency Syndrome). Over the years, the 
medical field has made significant progress in HIV treatment, making the 
virus manageable and improving the quality of life for those affected.

44 
 
By reading extensively, students can explore the science behind HIV and 
AIDS. They learn about the virus's impact on the body, methods of 
transmission, and the importance of prevention. This includes the role of 
antiretroviral therapy (ART), which suppresses the virus and allows 
individuals to lead healthy lives. 
Moreover, through personal narratives, secondary school students can 
better understand the social aspects of HIV and AIDS. They encounter 
stories of resilience, discrimination, and triumph. These stories break 
down stigma and encourage empathy toward affected individuals. 
Knowledge gained through extensive reading promotes tolerance, 
reduces misconceptions, and fosters inclusivity. 
Extensive reading also allows students to delve into the global and 
historical context of HIV and AIDS. They learn how different cultures 
address the epidemic, understand the role of international organisations 
in combating its spread, and recognise the value of collective effort in 
raising awareness and supporting affected communities. 
For secondary school students, reading widely about HIV and AIDS is 
not just about gathering facts. It is about developing a responsible 
attitude, taking active steps towards prevention, and supporting those 
living with the condition. Informed readers become advocates for positive 
change, emphasising the power of education in shaping a healthier and 
more compassionate world. 
(Adapted from ‘healtheducationresources.unesco.org’)

1.0. Introduction  
 Overview: Prepositions are words that establish relationships between nouns and 
other words in a sentence, typically to show direction, location, position, time, 
manner, or to introduce an object. Prepositions are essential components of English 
grammar because they act as linguistic connectors to clarify relationship, add 
precision, and enrich communication 
 General Competence(s):   
- 
Communication: 
 Express oneself (spoken and written) clearly and effectively 
 Speak effectively in public 
 Hook: Picture a cat hiding under the bed. Where is the cat? 
Key Terms  
 Preposition: word that show relationships between a noun or pronoun and other words in 
a sentence. 
 Object: the noun or pronoun that follows the preposition and completes its meaning 
 Collocation: phrases where certain words naturally pair with specific prepositions. 
2.0. SUB-TOPIC: TYPES OF PREPOSITIONS 
 Introduction: This topic introduces learners to types of prepositions. Teaching the types 
of prepositions (of time, place, direction …)  is aimed at helping learners understand how 
prepositions function in sentences.  
 Specific Competence: Use prepositions in sentences. 
3.0. Learning Activities:

Activity 1: Discussing prepositions

Passage

in a soft voice and gave him a booklet with information on how to stay safe. She also

 Explain to the learners that the words identified in the passage are called prepositions. 
 Give a definition. (Prepositions are words that establish relationships between nouns and

to 
introduce 
an 
object.)
Activity 2: Distinguishing types of prepositions: 
 Using the prepositions in the passage explain the types. 
i. 
Prepositions of time: These are prepositions that show the relationship between 
time and other parts of the sentence. They tell when something happens (at, on, 
before, after, during…)  
Example: During the talk the nurse gave him a booklet… 
  
ii. 
Prepositions of place:  These are words that show the location or position of 
something or someone in relation to another object or place (at, on, in, under…) 
Example: He sat on a bench under big tree … 
iii. 
Prepositions of direction: These are words that show movement from one place 
to another. They tell where something is going or in which direction it is moving 
(to, through…)  
Example: After the session, he walked through the village … 
 
iv. 
Prepositions of manner: These show how something happens or the way in 
which an action is done (with …) 
Example: The nurse spoke to him in a soft voice and gave him a booklet with …

 Prepositions are words that show relationships between other words and in a sentence, 
often related to time, place and direction.  
 Prepositions of time show when something happens 
 Prepositions of place indicate where something is located. 
 Prepositions of direction show movement or route. 
 Prepositions are essential to clarify relationship and to add conciseness in speech and 
writing.

STRUCTURE 2 
PHRASAL VERBS 
1.0. Introduction  
 Overview: Phrasal verbs are formed by combining verbs and one or more particles of 
prepositions, and sometimes of adverbs. Phrasal verbs form part of frequently used 
structures in English language conversation. They offer a variety of expressions, and 
make communication efficient. Learners will identify and use phrasal verbs correctly in 
sentences so that they can demonstrate effective spoken and written communication in 
English. 
 General Competence(s):   
- 
Communication: 
 Express oneself (spoken and written) clearly and effectively 
 Speak effectively in public 
 Hook: Do you know that the meaning of a simple verb can completely change when 
paired with a preposition? 
 Key Terms  
 Verb: the base action or state word. For example, run, jump, think. 
 Preposition: a word that shows a relationship between a noun or pronoun and another 
word in a sentence. It often indicates direction, place, time, cause, manner, or possession. 
 Particle: a small word that has a grammatical function that is used in combination with 
verbs to form phrasal verbs. It can either a preposition or an adverb that combines with 
the verb to form a phrasal verb. 
 Transitive phrasal verb: one that requires an object to complete its meaning. E.g. 
take off your hat, putt off the meeting, she turned down the offer. 
 Intransitive:  one that does not require an object to complete its meaning. E.g. he woke 
up late. 
 Separable: some phrasal verbs allow the object to split the verb and the particle. E.g.  
turn the lights off, and look after someone. 
 Inseparable: some phrasal verbs do not allow the object to split the verb and  
the particle.  
2.0. SUB-TOPIC: PHRASAL VERBS

52 
 
 Introduction: Phrasal verbs are a combination of verbs plus prepositions and/or verbs 
plus adverbs. This topic introduces learners to phrasal verbs and their uses in 
communication. Learning about phrasal verbs can enhance communication skills and

 Specific Competence: Use phrasal verbs in sentences.

 Overview: A summary is a brief and concise piece of writing that presents only 
main points from a given text (oral or written) without altering the meaning of the 
text. The ability to summarise a text is an important skill in academic and social 
contexts in which there is endless need to make information easier to 
communicate and understand.

- 
Critical thinking: 
 Analyse texts to improve the ability to understand.

KEY TERMS /WORDS/VOCABULARY  
• Paragraph: A group of sentences relating to a single idea  
• Paragraph summary: the process of shortening a passage by capturing only the 
important information.   
  Topic sentence: A sentence that states the main idea of a paragraph.  
3.0. SUB-TOPIC: PARAGRAPH SUMMARY 
 Introduction: A paragraph summary is a concise restatement of the key points of a 
passage. It preserves the original content of the passage without including unnecessary 
details, examples, or repetitions Summarising is an important skill that can improve 
reading comprehension and writing. By learning how to summarise paragraphs, we can 
better understand and communicate the core message of a text. This topic will focus on 
identifying the main idea, distinguishing important supporting details, and creating a 
concise summary that reflects the original passage’s meaning.

HIV, which stands for Human Immunodeficiency Virus, attacks the 
body’s immune system, 
making it harder to fight off infections and diseases. If left untreated, HIV 
can lead to AIDS 
(Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome), a more severe stage of the

breastfeeding. Although there is no cure, antiretroviral treatment allows 
people with HIV to live long and healthy lives.

prophylaxis (PrEP) and post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) are preventive 
medications that reduce the risk of contracting the virus. Regular testing, 
safe sex practices, and access to healthcare services all play a role in 
controlling the epidemic.

sustained public health initiatives remain essential in the fight against

Key Terms /Words/Vocabulary  
Stanza: A group of lines in a poem, forming a unit. 
Rhythm: The musical quality created by the pattern of stressed and unstressed syllables. 
Rhyme: Words that sound alike, especially at the ends of lines. 
Imagery: Descriptive language that creates vivid pictures in the reader’s mind. 
Tone: The mood or attitude conveyed by the poem. 
Theme: The central idea or message in the poem. 
Metaphor: A comparison without using “like” or “as.” 
Simile: A comparison using “like” or “as.” 
Symbolism: The use of symbols to represent ideas or qualities. 
Alliteration: Repetition of initial consonant sounds.

Read the passage below and answer the questions that follow. 
1. In today’s fast-changing world, knowing how to use digital technology is not just helpful—it 
is very important. Digital literacy means being able to find, understand, create, and share 
information using computers, smartphones, and the internet. It is not only about using a phone or 
computer, but also about thinking carefully, solving problems, and using the internet wisely.

62 
 
With digital literacy, people can find useful information, learn online, and talk to others around 
the world. But even though it has many advantages, digital literacy also brings some problems 
that need attention. 
2. One big problem on the internet is misinformation, which means wrong or false information. 
The internet has many websites, blogs, and social media posts. Some of them are not true. 
Some people or groups share fake news to change people’s opinions, cause fear, or push their 
own plans. A digitally literate person should always check if the information is true by looking at 
trusted sources like school websites, government sites, or good news channels. Without this 
skill, people may believe false information, which can be dangerous for everyone. 
3. Another important part of digital literacy is online safety. More people are using the internet 
for social media, online shopping, and banking. This gives cybercriminals (online thieves) more 
chances to steal from people. These criminals can hack accounts, steal personal information, or 
cheat people with fake offers. Many people share private details without knowing the danger. 
To stay safe, everyone should use strong passwords, turn on two-step verification (like a code 
sent to your phone), and be careful with links they click on. 
4. Digital literacy also helps people get better jobs and work faster. Today, many jobs need 
basic computer skills, like typing documents, using spreadsheets, or joining online meetings. 
Also, digital tools help business owners sell products to people in other places. Even farmers 
use digital tools to check the weather, see market prices, and learn better ways to farm. In 
hospitals, doctors use computers to keep patient records and talk to patients online. 
5. Even though digital literacy is needed more and more, many people still don’t have access to 
it. In rural areas, some people don’t have computers, internet, or good training. This makes it 
hard for them to learn and compete in today’s world. Governments and schools should work 
together to fix this problem by giving low-cost internet, teaching programs, and better digital 
tools. With these efforts, digital literacy can reach everyone and help both individuals and 
communities grow.
